message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 2/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible.

## Claim under review
"Trained blind/vision-impaired individuals can learn to comprehend accelerated (time-compressed) speech at rates up to ~22 syllables per second, roughly 3x the normal speaking rate of ~6 syl/s."

**Source:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3805979/ (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)"

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll verify this claim about accelerated speech comprehension in blind/vision-impaired individuals. Let me start by fetching the primary source.
Let me fetch the primary source and search for corroborating/contradicting evidence in parallel.

input.prompt: Find any statement about the rate at which blind or vision-impaired individuals can comprehend accelerated/time-compressed speech. Specifically: what syllables-per-second rate is mentioned (e.g. 22 syl/s), what is stated as the normal speaking rate (e.g. 6 syl/s), and the exact wording. Quote the relevant sentences verbatim. Also note the publication date and journal.

input.query: blind individuals comprehend accelerated time-compressed speech 22 syllables per second

content.content:
# Speech Comprehension Rates in Vision-Impaired Individuals

**Publication:** Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, October 23, 2013

**Key Statement on Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension:**

"vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)—exceeding by far the capacities of normal sighted listeners whose upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s"

**Additional Context:**

The study notes that untrained listeners typically comprehend speech at approximately 8 syllables per second, while vision-impaired subjects in the study trained to understand ultra-fast speech at rates of 18 syl/s, achieving around 70% comprehension after approximately 6 months of training using screen-reader text-to-speech devices.

Web search results for query: "blind individuals comprehend accelerated time-compressed speech 22 syllables per second"

Links: [{"title":"V10. Learning to comprehend time-compressed speech affects premotor and cerebellar regions in late-blind and normally sighted subjects - ScienceDirect","url":"https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1388245715003351"},{"title":"Frontiers | Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full"},{"title":"V10. Learning to comprehend time-compressed speech affects premotor and cerebellar regions in late-blind and normally sighted subjects | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/279737259_V10_Learning_to_comprehend_time-compressed_speech_affects_premotor_and_cerebellar_regions_in_late-blind_and_normally_sighted_subjects"},{"title":"(PDF) Why do blind listeners use visual cortex for understanding ultra‐fast speech?","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/224881917_Why_do_blind_listeners_use_visual_cortex_for_understanding_ultra-fast_speech"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PMC","url":"https://ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3805979"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study | BMC Neuroscience | Full Text","url":"https://bmcneurosci.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/1471-2202-14-74"},{"title":"Comprehension of ultra-fast speech-blind vs.\" normally hearing\" persons | Request PDF","url":"https://www.researchgate.net/publication/228846770_Comprehension_of_ultra-fast_speech-blind_vs_normally_hearing_persons"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar – a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study","url":"https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC3847124/"},{"title":"Ultra-fast speech comprehension in blind subjects engages primary visual cortex, fusiform gyrus, and pulvinar - a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23879896/"}]

Here are the search results for your query about blind individuals and time-compressed speech:

Individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second (syl/s), exceeding by far the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (approximately 8 syl/s).

In addition to classical language zones (left inferior frontal gyrus, bilateral middle temporal gyrus), the right visual cortex (V1), left supplementary motor area (SMA), and cerebellum contribute to the processing of accelerated speech in blind subjects.

Blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar.

Patients suffering from vision impairments may considerably benefit during academic education from screen-reading text-to-speech systems operating at ultra-fast syllable rates.

The research indicates that this is a remarkable skill developed through training and exposure to accelerated speech, involving cross-modal neuroplasticity where visual cortex regions are recruited to support auditory processing.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: The primary source is confirmed. Now let me check the claim's two key numbers against the source. The claim says "roughly 3x the normal speaking rate of ~6 syl/s" — but the source says the untrained/normal upper limit is ~8 syl/s, not 6 syl/s. Let me verify the "normal speaking rate" figure and whether the source distinguishes between "normal speaking rate" and "untrained comprehension limit."

input.prompt: What does the paper state about: (1) the maximum rate vision-impaired/blind individuals can comprehend accelerated speech (syllables per second)? (2) the normal/natural speaking rate in syllables per second? (3) the upper comprehension limit of normal sighted/untrained listeners in syllables per second? Quote exact sentences with numbers. Distinguish carefully between "speaking rate", "comprehension limit of untrained listeners", and "trained blind comprehension rate".

input.query: normal conversational speaking rate syllables per second average speech

content.content:
# Speech Rate Comprehension Findings

Based on the paper's statements:

**(1) Maximum rate for vision-impaired/blind individuals:**
"vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)"

**(2) Normal speaking rate:**
The paper does not explicitly define a standard natural speaking rate in syllables per second.

**(3) Upper comprehension limit of normal sighted/untrained listeners:**
"the maximum performance level of untrained listeners (ca. 8 syl/s)"

Also stated: "the upper limit is at ca. 8 syl/s"

---

**Key distinction:** The paper differentiates these as separate phenomena—trained blind individuals can reach ~22 syl/s, while untrained sighted listeners plateau around 8 syl/s, representing a substantial gap in processing capacity after specialized training versus natural listening ability.

Web search results for query: "normal conversational speaking rate syllables per second average speech"

Links: [{"title":"Speaking Speeds in Different languages","url":"https://voiceoverkickstart.com/speaking-speeds-in-different-languages/"},{"title":"Comparison of Speech Rate and Long-Term Average Speech Spectrum between Korean Clear Speech and Conversational Speech - PMC","url":"https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6773961/"},{"title":"Speech tempo - Wikipedia","url":"https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speech_tempo"},{"title":"Calculating Cluttering Speech Rate: An Important Core Symptom.","url":"https://toofastforwords.com/symptoms-cluttering/main/speech-rate/"},{"title":"Methods and apparatus for semantic unit based automatic indexing and searching in data archive systems","url":"https://image-ppubs.uspto.gov/dirsearch-public/print/downloadPdf/7177795"},{"title":"How Fast Does the Average Person Speak? - Word Counter Blog","url":"https://wordcounter.net/blog/2016/06/02/101702_how-fast-average-person-speaks.html"},{"title":"Speaking rate characteristics of elementary-school-aged children","url":"https://moody.utexas.edu/sites/default/files/Speaking%20rate%20characteristics%20of%20elementary%20school-aged%20children_2010.pdf"},{"title":"Gender Bias in Instruction-Guided Speech Synthesis Models","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2502.05649"},{"title":"Human speech may have a universal transmission rate: 39 bits per second | Science | AAAS","url":"https://www.science.org/content/article/human-speech-may-have-universal-transmission-rate-39-bits-second"},{"title":"Rhythm Modeling for Voice Conversion","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2307.06040"}]

Based on the search results, here's what research shows about normal conversational speaking rates:

A typical speaking rate for English is 4 syllables per second, but in different emotional or social contexts the rate may vary, with one study reporting a range between 3.3 and 5.9 syllables per second. Additionally, most people speak at an average speed of four to five syllables per second, with most words being two to three syllables long, giving an average of approximately 100–130 words per minute.

When you're just having a casual chat, you'll usually speak at a rate of 110 to 130 words per minute.

It's worth noting that speech rate varies across different contexts. For example, neuroscience research indicates an upper bound for auditory processing of 9 syllables per second in US English – any faster and our brains can't convert our ideas into speech.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I now have enough to evaluate the claim carefully. Let me analyze the components.

The core claim has three assertions:
1. **Trained blind/vision-impaired individuals can comprehend accelerated speech at ~22 syl/s** — DIRECTLY SUPPORTED by the source verbatim quote.
2. **Normal speaking rate is ~6 syl/s** — NOT from the source. The source never states a "normal speaking rate" of 6 syl/s. Independent literature puts normal conversational speaking rate at ~4-5 syl/s (typically 4 syl/s for English). 6 syl/s is on the high end. The source's "8 syl/s" figure is the *comprehension upper limit of untrained listeners*, not a *speaking rate*.
3. **"roughly 3x the normal speaking rate"** — 22 ÷ 6 = 3.67x, which is closer to 4x than 3x. And 22 ÷ 4 (true normal rate) = 5.5x. The "3x" framing is mathematically loose and based on an unsupported 6 syl/s baseline.

Let me verify the source isn't disputed and check the 6 syl/s figure origin more precisely.

input.query: ultra-fast speech blind 22 syllables per second criticism replication time-compressed speech comprehension limit

a functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/23879896/"},{"title":"Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans - PubMed","url":"https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24167485/"},{"title":"Frontiers | Training of ultra-fast speech comprehension induces functional reorganization of the central-visual system in late-blind humans","url":"https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full"}]

Based on the search results, here's what I found about ultra-fast speech, blind listeners, and the 22 syllables per second finding:

## Ultra-Fast Speech Comprehension in Blind Listeners

Blind individuals suffering from vision loss of a peripheral origin may learn to understand spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables per second - exceeding by far the maximum performance level of normal-sighted listeners (approximately 8 syl/s).

## Brain Mechanisms

Blind people highly proficient in ultra-fast speech perception showed significant hemodynamic activation of right-hemispheric primary visual cortex (V1), contralateral fusiform gyrus (FG), and bilateral pulvinar (Pv).

## Training and Reproducibility

A training study addressed whether acquisition of ultra-fast (18 syl/s) speech perception skills induces de novo central-visual hemodynamic activation in late-blind participants. Additionally, researchers asked to what extent subjects with normal or residual vision can improve understanding of accelerated verbal utterances by means of specific training measures.

## Time-Compressed Speech Comprehension Testing

Verbal utterances were played to participants at different speaking rates amounting from 6 syl/s up to 22 syl/s, using a manual staircase procedure in order to determine the actual syllable rate at which repetition performance amounted to about 80%.

The research indicates that this is a reproducible finding in controlled studies with documented measurement procedures, though the search results did not specifically highlight criticisms or debates about the replication of these findings.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text:
I have enough evidence to render a verdict. Let me analyze adversarially.

**What's confirmed:**
- The ~22 syl/s figure for trained vision-impaired individuals is directly and verbatim supported by the primary source (Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013, PMC3805979). This is a peer-reviewed primary source — appropriate quality.
- The finding is reproduced across multiple papers by the same/related research groups (BMC Neuroscience 2013, Moos & Trouvain). No credible source disputes the ~22 syl/s figure.
- Interestingly, the source's testing range was literally "from 6 syl/s up to 22 syl/s" — so 6 syl/s does appear in the paper, but as the LOW END of the stimulus staircase, NOT as a defined "normal speaking rate."

**Where the claim overreaches:**

1. **The "~6 syl/s normal speaking rate" baseline is NOT supported by the source.** The source explicitly states the untrained *comprehension* upper limit is "ca. 8 syl/s" — and never defines a "normal speaking rate" of 6 syl/s. Independent literature puts the actual normal conversational speaking rate at ~4-5 syl/s (typically 4 syl/s for English). The claim conflates/invents a "6 syl/s" baseline that the source does not state.

2. **The "roughly 3x" multiplier is mathematically wrong on its own terms.** 22 ÷ 6 = 3.67x (closer to 4x). Against the true normal speaking rate of ~4 syl/s, it's ~5.5x. Against the source's own stated untrained limit of 8 syl/s, it's ~2.75x. The "3x" framing doesn't cleanly follow from any of these and rests on an unsupported baseline.

3. **The supporting quote only covers the first half of the claim** (the 22 syl/s figure). It says nothing about "6 syl/s normal speaking rate" or "3x." So per checklist item #1, the quote does NOT support the full claim as stated — the comparison/multiplier portion is an unsupported addition.

The headline number (~22 syl/s for trained blind individuals) is genuinely well-sourced. But the claim as written wraps that fact in a fabricated baseline (6 syl/s "normal speaking rate," which the source never asserts) and a loose/incorrect multiplier ("3x"). Under the adversarial standard — where the quote must support the claim and invented figures count against it — the claim is partially refuted: the core fact survives, but the comparative framing is unsupported and inaccurate.

input.evidence: The PRIMARY fact (trained vision-impaired individuals reaching ~22 syl/s) IS verbatim-supported by the source (PMC3805979, Frontiers in Human Neuroscience, 2013): "vision-impaired individuals may learn to understand accelerated spoken language at a rate of up to about 22 syllables (syl) per second (s)." This is reproduced across multiple peer-reviewed papers (BMC Neuroscience 2013; Moos & Trouvain) with no credible dispute, so the headline number is sound. HOWEVER the claim's comparative framing is NOT supported and is partly fabricated: (1) The source NEVER states a "normal speaking rate of ~6 syl/s." It states the untrained-listener COMPREHENSION upper limit is "ca. 8 syl/s" (a different construct from speaking rate). The "6 syl/s" actually appears in the paper only as the LOW END of the stimulus staircase ("from 6 syl/s up to 22 syl/s"), not as a normal speaking rate. (2) Independent literature puts the true normal conversational speaking rate at ~4-5 syl/s (typically ~4 syl/s for English), not 6. (3) The "roughly 3x" multiplier is mathematically off: 22/6 = 3.67x (~4x); against the real ~4 syl/s rate it is ~5.5x; against the source's own 8 syl/s untrained limit it is ~2.75x — "3x" follows from none of these cleanly. (4) The supporting quote covers only the 22 syl/s half and says nothing about "6 syl/s" or "3x," so the comparison is an unsupported addition. Net: the core fact is valid but the claim as written embeds an invented baseline and an inaccurate multiplier.
input.counterSource: https://www.frontiersin.org/articles/10.3389/fnhum.2013.00701/full (source states untrained limit "ca. 8 syl/s", never "6 syl/s normal speaking rate"); en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Speech_tempo and speech-rate literature (normal conversational rate ~4-5 syl/s, not 6)

content.text:
Verdict submitted: **refuted=true** (high confidence).

The ~22 syl/s figure for trained vision-impaired individuals is genuinely well-sourced and reproduced across peer-reviewed papers. But the claim as written is refuted because its comparative framing is unsupported and inaccurate: the source never states a "normal speaking rate of ~6 syl/s" (it states an *untrained comprehension limit* of ~8 syl/s, a different construct), the true normal conversational speaking rate is ~4-5 syl/s, and the "roughly 3x" multiplier is mathematically loose (22/6 = 3.67x; vs. real rate ~5.5x). The supporting quote only covers the 22 syl/s half, so the baseline and multiplier are unsupported additions.
